localeHint: You can switch languages by setting the NEXT_LOCALE cookie to en, es, he, or ar.
languageSettingsNote: Your language is saved so the site opens automatically in your chosen language next time too.

productGuide.eyebrow: Build with AI in a structured way
productGuide.title: Build with AI without creating a mess
productGuide.subtitle: This starter is built for product builders, founders, and designers who want to ship real products with AI tools like Claude Code or Cursor without getting slowed down by setup choices, inconsistent code, or architectural drift. You do not need to understand every file before you start. Most of the infrastructure is already in place. CLAUDE.md tells your AI assistant exactly how the project works, which patterns to use, which files to create, where to put things — so you don't have to explain it on every prompt.

shadcnUi.title: UI components (shadcn/ui)
shadcnUi.description: This project uses shadcn/ui for buttons, dialogs, forms, and many other building blocks. Components live in your codebase (not an opaque package), so you can customize them freely.
shadcnUi.install: Install additional components from the official catalog with the CLI, for example: pnpm dlx shadcn@latest add accordion — run the command in the project root and pick components from the docs.

purpose.title: Why this website exists
purpose.description: The goal is to help you build faster with AI without giving up structure, clarity, and long-term quality.

fast.title: Move faster
fast.description: Start from a ready-made foundation instead of building every screen and folder from scratch.

tokenSavings.title: Save AI tokens
tokenSavings.description: AI does not need to build everything from scratch because it already has context, ready-to-use components, and a clear way of working.

organized.title: Stay organized
organized.description: UI, forms, server calls, translations, and shared state already follow a clear structure.

conventions.title: Work with good conventions
conventions.description: The foundation gives AI a better framework, so the code it produces is usually cleaner, more consistent, and easier to continue working with.

handoff.title: Let a developer continue after you
handoff.description: A human developer can read the code, improve it, and keep building without starting over.

aiReady.title: AI-ready context
aiReady.description: CLAUDE.md and .claude/rules/ give your AI assistant complete project context so it follows the right patterns without being told each time.

goodFit.title: When this is a great fit
goodFit.description: This foundation is a strong fit when both AI speed and output quality matter to you.

founder.title: A founder building an MVP with AI
founder.description: Great for a non-technical founder who wants to launch a serious first version quickly.

designer.title: A designer building a real product
designer.description: Great for designers who want to move from an idea to a product that actually works.

developer.title: A developer who wants a structured foundation
developer.description: Useful for developers who do not want to redefine everything from scratch in every project.

team.title: A team that wants consistency
team.description: Helpful for teams that want consistency across UI, forms, API calls, translations, and state management.

lessSuitable.title: When this is less suitable
lessSuitable.description: Not every project needs this kind of foundation, especially when future maintenance is not important.

randomPages.title: If the goal is just to generate pages quickly
randomPages.description: If structure does not matter to you, this foundation may feel more opinionated than you need.

landingPage.title: If you only need a landing page
landingPage.description: For a simple landing page, you probably do not need a solution this structured and comprehensive.

throwaway.title: If it is a one-off experiment
throwaway.description: For a small project that only exists for a short demo, you may not need all of these conventions.

noConventions.title: If long-term code quality does not matter
noConventions.description: If you do not care whether the code is readable, consistent, and easy to continue from, this is probably more than you need.

howToUse.title: How to get it running on your computer
howToUse.description: Check the prerequisites first, then follow the 3 steps below.

prerequisites.title: Before you start
prerequisites.description: Open your Terminal and run each command below. If you see a version number, you already have it. If you get an error, click the install link.

git.name: Git
git.description: Needed to download the project to your computer.

node.name: Node.js
node.description: Needed to run the project locally.

pnpm.name: pnpm
pnpm.description: The package manager this project uses.

prerequisites.editorDescription: You need an editor to open and work on the project files.

success.title: It worked! Your app is running.
success.body: If a webpage appeared at http://localhost:3000 — you're done! Your app is now running on your own computer. You can start exploring or building right away.

troubleshooting.title: Something didn't work?
troubleshooting.description: Here are the most common problems people run into, and exactly how to fix each one.

pnpmNotFound.problem: "command not found: pnpm" or "pnpm is not recognized"
pnpmNotFound.fix: Node.js is not installed yet. Go back to Step 1 and install it. After installing, close your terminal completely, reopen it, and try the command again.

portInUse.fix: Another app is using that slot. Close other running programs, then try pnpm dev again. Or use a different port with this command:

moduleNotFound.problem: "Cannot find module" or errors during install
moduleNotFound.fix: Run pnpm install again (Step 3), then try pnpm dev again. If it still fails, delete the node_modules folder and run pnpm install one more time.

envError.problem: App opens but shows a settings or config error
envError.fix: Open the .env.local file in your editor. The NEXT_PUBLIC_SERVER_URL line can be left blank for local testing — save the file, stop the server with Ctrl+C, and run pnpm dev again.

installTools.title: Install Git, Node.js, pnpm, and an IDE
installTools.description: Before you start, make sure the core tools are installed on your computer. You can use the official install pages, and for pnpm you can also install it quickly with npm. For the editor itself, VS Code and Cursor are both great options.

cloneProject.title: Create your own copy on GitHub and download it
cloneProject.description: Use the GitHub template to create your own repository, then download it to your computer with git clone.
cloneProject.githubNote: You need a free GitHub account. Don't have one? Create it at github.com — it takes 2 minutes.
cloneProject.commandNote: After creating your repo on GitHub, click the green 'Code' button, copy the URL, and replace <your-repository-url> in the command below.

openProject.title: Open the project in your editor and install dependencies
openProject.description: Open the project folder in your editor, then run the install command so everything is ready on your computer.
openProject.commandNote: Tip: VS Code and Cursor have a built-in terminal. Open it from the menu: View → Terminal. It will automatically be inside your project folder.

startProject.title: Start the app
startProject.description: Run the dev server and open the app in your browser at localhost:3000.
startProject.commandNote: The second command copies your local settings file (.env.local) when you're ready. You can skip it for a first run — add it if the app asks for configuration.
startProject.note: After pnpm dev, once the project is running, you can open it in your browser at http://localhost:3000.

resources.title: Useful links
resources.description: Here you can find the project link itself, along with the installation pages for the main tools and editors.

github.title: GitHub repository
github.description: This is the main link to the template. You can update it later if the repository changes.

git.title: Install Git
git.description: You need Git to clone the project to your computer and keep your own version.

node.title: Install Node.js
node.description: You need Node.js to run the project locally if it is not installed yet.

pnpm.title: Install pnpm
pnpm.description: This foundation uses pnpm, and you can install it from the official page or quickly with npm.

vscode.title: Download VS Code
vscode.description: A popular editor with a large extension ecosystem and a familiar workflow.

cursor.title: Download Cursor
cursor.description: An AI-first editor that feels similar to VS Code and works well for guided building.

claudeCode.title: Download Claude Code
claudeCode.description: Claude Code is an AI coding assistant that understands this project's structure and writes better code.

githubSignup.title: Create a free GitHub account
githubSignup.description: You need a GitHub account to create your own copy of the project template.

checklist.title: Get started in minutes
checklist.description: Check off each item as you go — your progress is saved right here in your browser.
checklist.allDone: You're all set! Your app is running.
checklist.bonusDescription: Not required to run the app, but makes AI-powered building much better.

terminal.explanation: A window where you type short text commands. Think of it as texting your computer instead of clicking buttons. On Mac: Cmd + Space → type Terminal. On Windows: press the Windows key → type cmd.

lts.explanation: Long Term Support — the safe, well-tested version recommended for most people. Not the newest release, but the one with the fewest surprises. Always pick LTS when installing Node.js.

packageManager.explanation: A tool that downloads all the code libraries your app needs in one command. Run `pnpm install` once and it fetches everything automatically — like an ingredient auto-delivery for your project.

codeEditor.explanation: The app where you open and edit the project files — like Google Docs, but for code. VS Code and Cursor are both free and has AI built in, so you can chat with it while you build.

github.explanation: A website for storing and sharing code projects online. You'll click 'Use this template' to create your own personal copy of this project, then download it to your computer to start building.

localhost.explanation: Your own computer acting as a website server. When you run `pnpm dev`, your app opens at localhost:3000 — it looks and works just like a real website, but only you can see it.

envFile.explanation: A hidden file that stores app configuration like server addresses. This project ships a ready-to-copy example file — you run one command to create yours, and you won't need to change anything for local testing.

git.explanation: Version control software: it records snapshots of your project over time so you can track changes, go back if needed, and sync code with teammates or sites like GitHub.

javascript.explanation: The programming language this project is written in for the browser and server tools. Node.js is a runtime that runs JavaScript on your computer so your app can build and run locally.

git.title: Git
git.description: <git>Git</git> is a version control system — the essential tool for tracking changes and managing your project's history. Open <terminal>Terminal</terminal> and run the check command — if you see a version number, you're good.

node.title: Node.js
node.description: <javascript>JavaScript</javascript> runtime environment needed to run this project on your computer. Download the <lts>LTS</lts> (recommended) version from the official page.

pnpm.title: pnpm
pnpm.description: The <packageManager>package manager</packageManager> this project uses. If you have Node.js, you can install it instantly with a single command.

editor.title: Code editor
editor.description: You need a <codeEditor>code editor</codeEditor> to open and work on the project files. VS Code and Cursor are both free and great options.

githubAccount.title: GitHub account + your own copy
githubAccount.description: Create a free <github>GitHub</github> account if you don't have one. Then open the project page and click "Use this template" to create your personal copy.

clone.title: Clone the project to your computer
clone.description: On <github>GitHub</github>, click the green Code button, copy the URL, then run this command in <terminal>Terminal</terminal> — replace the placeholder with your actual URL.

install.title: Install project dependencies
install.description: Open <terminal>Terminal</terminal> inside the project folder (in VS Code or Cursor: View → Terminal) and run this command. It downloads everything the project needs.

env.title: Copy the settings file
env.description: Do this after the app is running (or if you see a configuration message). Creates your local <envFile>.env.local</envFile> from the example — you don't need to edit it for local testing.

run.title: Start the app
run.description: Run this command and your app will open in the browser at <localhost>localhost:3000</localhost>. You should see this page — you're done!

claudeCode.title: Claude Code
claudeCode.description: An AI assistant that reads this project's CLAUDE.md and writes code that follows the right patterns. Dramatically speeds up building.

cursorBonus.title: Cursor editor
cursorBonus.description: An AI-first <codeEditor>code editor</codeEditor> built for coding with AI. Very similar to VS Code — if you haven't picked an editor yet, start here.

builtWith.title: Credits / Built with
builtWith.description: This foundation is based on modern tools that help you work faster, more cleanly, and with better conventions from day one.

nextjs.name: Next.js
nextjs.description: The main framework that handles pages, navigation, and a production-ready app structure.

react.name: React
react.description: The library used to build the product's interactive interface.

shadcn.name: shadcn/ui
shadcn.description: A high-quality component set for building a clean, modern, and consistent interface.

tailwind.name: Tailwind CSS
tailwind.description: A styling system that helps you move fast while keeping the design consistent.

typescript.name: TypeScript
typescript.description: Adds structure and safety to the code so it is easier to maintain over time.

nextIntl.name: next-intl
nextIntl.description: Handles translations and multilingual support inside the app.

zustand.name: Zustand
zustand.description: A simple and practical tool for managing shared app data.

zod.name: Zod
zod.description: Validates data clearly so forms and logic are more reliable.

reactHookForm.name: React Hook Form
reactHookForm.description: The form system used in this foundation, with less repetitive code.

recharts.name: Recharts
recharts.description: The charting library used for dashboards and data visualizations in the demo.

swr.name: SWR
swr.description: Handles data fetching and caching in a modern and convenient way.

vercel.name: Vercel
vercel.description: A deployment platform that works especially well with Next.js projects.

ignoreAtFirst.title: What you can safely ignore at first
ignoreAtFirst.description: This repo has many files. Most are infrastructure you never touch, or optional features you can delete.
ignoreAtFirst.doNotRemove: Do not remove: src/app/api/proxy/, src/lib/, src/constants/, src/components/form/, or messages/en.json — those are load-bearing.

addFeature.title: How to add a new feature
addFeature.note: The AI will know how to do it for you, this is just a manual explaination.
addFeature.description: The contact form at src/features/contact/ is the canonical example. Follow the same steps for any new feature.

coreVsOptional.title: Core vs Optional
coreVsOptional.coreDescription: Load-bearing infrastructure. Do not remove these files.
coreVsOptional.optionalDescription: Safe to delete or adapt when building your real app.

mistakes.title: Common mistakes this starter helps you avoid
mistakes.description: These are the mistakes AI tools tend to make in unstructured codebases. The conventions here prevent most of them.

productGuide.devReference: Developer reference (skip if you're not a programmer)

guide.heroDescription: A ready-to-use foundation for building websites and apps in a clean, organized, and scalable way. Especially useful for working with AI tools, so even non-technical users can build faster without ending up with messy code that developers struggle to maintain later.

whatsIncluded.title: What's included
whatsIncluded.description: Eight ready-made systems to help you start building right away without reinventing everything.

hooks.title: 19 ready-made hooks
hooks.description: Reusable pieces of code that save time on common frontend patterns.

forms.title: Form system
forms.description: Structured forms with validation, ready-made fields, and built-in translations.

api.title: Server connection
api.description: A clean and secure way to connect to your backend, with handling for requests, errors, and loading states.

ui.title: Interface components
ui.description: Ready-made components like buttons, tables, dialogs, charts, and animations.

i18n.title: Multilingual
i18n.description: Full support for multiple languages, including language switching and proper RTL layouts.

state.title: State management
state.description: A structured way to manage shared data and settings in the app without creating code chaos.

utilities.title: Utilities
utilities.description: Helpful functions and tools that save time and keep your code consistent.

loading.title: Loading states
loading.description: Clear and consistent loading states so the experience feels polished on every screen.

goldenRules.title: 13 golden rules
goldenRules.description: These rules help keep your code clean, consistent, and easy to maintain.

quickReference.title: Quick reference
quickReference.description: A quick map showing where each part lives in the project.

gettingStarted.title: Getting started
gettingStarted.description: Three simple steps to start working on your project.

template.title: Use the template
template.description: Create a copy of the template so you can start from a clean and organized project.

install.title: Install and set up
install.description: Install what you need and configure the project environment file.

build.title: Start building
build.description: Run the project and start building on top of a foundation that is already set up the right way.

dashboard.animatedNumbersDescription: Count-up animation with support for different display formats.

tokenUsage.title: AI tips
tokenUsage.description: Curated community tools and resources that work well with AI-assisted coding. None of these ship with this app or with Claude by default—each link goes to an independent project so you can read how it works and decide if it fits your workflow.
tokenUsage.disclaimer: Third-party and community projects only. Anthropic and this template do not maintain them. Review licenses, permissions, and install steps before use, and only install what you trust.
tokenUsage.cavemanDescription: A community skill and plugin set that shortens agent replies in a structured way (often much fewer tokens) while keeping technical detail. Available for Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, Cursor, and others—see the install table below and the README on GitHub.

claudeMem.title: Claude Mem
claudeMem.description: Community Claude Code plugin that stores session context over time so you can resume work with less re-explaining.
claudeMem.whyItMatters: Long projects accumulate decisions and conventions that are easy to lose between sessions. A dedicated memory layer keeps that context discoverable instead of retyping it.
useCases: Keeping team conventions and file layout notes handy
Getting back to speed after a break
examples: Resume where we left off and pull in anything relevant from recent work on this repo.
Remember the main architecture and how we format API errors in this project.

uiUxProMax.title: UI UX Pro Max
uiUxProMax.description: Community skill and tooling focused on UI craft—spacing, type scale, hierarchy, and patterns—so generated interfaces feel more deliberate than generic placeholders.
uiUxProMax.whyItMatters: Models can ship UIs fast, but they often look same-y. A design-oriented skill nudges output toward clearer hierarchy and more consistent polish.
useCases: Modern product chrome without a full design handoff
examples: Tighten this page layout so it feels more polished and current, without changing the underlying behavior.
Adjust spacing, typography, and hierarchy on this dashboard so scanning is easier.

awesomeClaudeCode.title: Awesome Claude Code
awesomeClaudeCode.description: Community-maintained list of Claude Code-related tools: skills, hooks, commands, plugins, and workflow notes.
awesomeClaudeCode.whyItMatters: Before writing your own Glue code, it helps to see what others already use. Lists like this are a low-noise way to discover patterns that survived real projects.
useCases: Browsing tools before building custom setup
Finding skills for UI, docs, or automation
Learning what other teams standardize on
examples: Find community skills that help with front-end polish.
Scan for memory, hooks, or task automation ideas I can adopt without reinventing them.

forms.formattedInputsHint: Every field uses a different formatter. All are required — submit empty to see each error. This button uses a dots loader and simulates a 1.5s network delay (button disabled while “saving“).
forms.textInputsHint: Name, email, and notes are all required — you will see several validation messages at once.
forms.selectSectionHint: Only category and status are required. Multi-select is optional.
forms.otpSectionHint: Enter the full code and check the terms box to submit.
forms.dateFileSectionHint: Pick a date and attach at least one file. The date range is optional.
forms.submitSuccessDescription: You can see the submitted values in the console.

dialogs.areYouSure: Are you sure you want to continue?
dialogs.deleteItemPermanently: This item will be permanently deleted.
dialogs.reusableDialog: A reusable dialog with a trigger, action buttons, and different sizes.
dialogs.nestedBody: You can use this pattern for confirmations, step-by-step flows, or forms inside a modal.
dialogs.overlayDescription: A full loading overlay appears automatically while the system is making background requests.

typography.title: Typography
typography.description: Sample copy uses Latin lorem ipsum so you can compare heading and body styles. Switch language to see locale-specific fonts. Theme and language controls stay in the header.

fontsNote.title: Fonts in this demo
fontsNote.latin: English and Spanish: Space Grotesk for headings, Plus Jakarta Sans for body text.
fontsNote.hebrew: Hebrew: Heebo for headings and body.
fontsNote.arabic: Arabic: Cairo for headings and body.

shadcn.title: shadcn/ui components
shadcn.description: Primitives such as buttons, cards, and form controls are based on shadcn/ui—accessible, Tailwind-styled components you copy into src/components/ui and can edit anytime.
shadcn.install: To add more: open the docs, choose a component, and run the CLI in the project root (for example: pnpm dlx shadcn@latest add dialog).

lorem.heading: Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet
lorem.short: Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.
lorem.body: Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat.
lorem.caption: Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt.
lorem.label: Lorem ipsum label

showcase.iconifyCatalogIntro: Browse thousands of icon sets and names at
showcase.lottieHint: Lightweight motion for loading and delight. If the file fails to load, a fallback spinner appears.
showcase.kbdHint: Use for shortcuts and key hints.
showcase.tabAlphaBody: First panel: lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.
showcase.tabBetaBody: Second panel: same type styles, different copy for comparison.
showcase.tabGammaBody: Third panel: keeps spacing and rhythm consistent across states.
showcase.accordion1Content1: Use consistent padding and gaps so dense UIs still feel calm. Prefer tokens from the theme over one-off pixel values.
showcase.accordion1Content2: Pair heading scale with body text: one clear title, supporting copy at a smaller step, and captions for metadata.
showcase.accordion2Content1: Reserve strong colors for actions and states. Muted foreground keeps secondary text readable without competing with primary content.
showcase.accordion2Content2: Test interactive states in both themes. Focus rings and borders should remain visible when switching themes.
showcase.accordion3Content1: Keep motion purposeful: short durations for feedback, avoid animating large layout shifts unless necessary.
showcase.accordion3Content2: Prefer native focus order, labels on controls, and sufficient touch targets—especially for drawers, sheets, and dialogs.
showcase.tooltipContent: Tooltips give quick context without cluttering the layout. They work with keyboard focus too.
showcase.popoverBody: Popovers hold a bit more content than tooltips and stay open until you click away.
showcase.drawerBody: Drawers slide up from the bottom on mobile. Good for actions and short forms.
showcase.resizableScrollSeparatorHint: Scroll area and separator in one split; drag the handle to resize. On small screens the split stacks vertically.
showcase.sheetBody: Sheets slide in from the edge. Useful for navigation and secondary content.
showcase.skeletonLoaderHint: The loading indicator reflects the global loader when this demo key is active.
showcase.sliderHint: Disabled here so this page stays view-only.
showcase.breathingHint: A light CSS keyframe loop—useful for idle highlights, attention cues, or subtle “live“ affordances.
showcase.animatedNumberHint: Counts from zero with easing—suitable for dashboards, KPIs, and stats that update when data changes.
showcase.emptyDescription: Empty states explain what happened and what the user can do next when there is no data.
showcase.termTipHint: Hover over (or tap) the underlined terms below to see their definition.
showcase.defSwr: A React data-fetching library. Stands for Stale-While-Revalidate — returns cached data immediately, then quietly refetches fresh data in the background.
showcase.defZustand: A lightweight global state library for React. Much simpler than Redux — no actions, reducers, or boilerplate. Just a store and a hook.
showcase.defRhf: A performant form library for React. Uses uncontrolled inputs and refs to minimize re-renders and keep large forms fast.
showcase.defZod: A TypeScript-first schema validation library. Define the shape of your data once and get both runtime validation and compile-time type inference for free.

landing.heroBadge: Every section here is easily reusable & customizable
landing.heroBackgroundAlt: Stylized view of Earth from space with glowing network lines.
landing.feature1Desc: react-hook-form + Zod + shared field components. One pattern for every form.
landing.feature2Desc: SWR-powered useFetch and useMutation. No boilerplate loading state.
landing.feature3Desc: next-intl with cookie-based locale switching. All strings centralized.
landing.ctaSubtitle: Clone the skeleton and start your next project in minutes.
landing.faqSubtitle: Straight answers about the template — customization, i18n, data, and auth.
landing.faq1A: It is a Next.js starter with shared UI, forms, data-fetching conventions, i18n, and routing patterns so you can focus on product features instead of wiring basics from scratch.
landing.faq2Q: Can I remove or replace the demo pages?
landing.faq2A: Yes. Demo routes live under the app demo folder with matching tab components. Remove them or swap the content when you are ready — the rest of the skeleton stays useful.
landing.faq3A: Copy is stored in locale files and loaded with next-intl. Add keys for new strings in each locale, and use the project translation helpers from components — do not hardcode user-visible text in JSX.
landing.faq4Q: How should I load and update data?
landing.faq4A: Use the shared useFetch hook for reads and useMutation for writes. They wrap the data layer with consistent loading and error handling — avoid calling fetch or axios directly inside components.
landing.faq5A: There is an auth route group and layout patterns you can extend. Connect your real identity provider or session layer to match production — the skeleton focuses on UI and app structure.
landing.heroSlide2Title: Form system, out of the box
landing.heroSlide3Desc: next-intl with RTL support and 4 locales built in.
landing.heroSlide4Desc: CLAUDE.md gives your AI full project context.
landing.splitNarrativeTitle: From a pretty page to a real product foundation
landing.splitNarrativeStep1Desc: A landing page should explain why your product matters before it explains every feature.
landing.splitNarrativeStep2Desc: Hero, proof, problem, product, pricing, FAQ — each block is reusable, typed, and easy to move.
landing.splitNarrativeStep3Desc: Your landing page should not be a throwaway page. It should live beside your routes, auth, forms, dashboard, and future product flows.
landing.splitNarrativeStep4Desc: As the product grows, the marketing system grows with it — new sections, new presets, new pages, same architecture.

errors.passwordMin8: Password must contain at least 8 characters
errors.passwordMax64: Password can contain up to 64 characters
errors.passwordUppercase: Password must include at least one uppercase letter
errors.passwordLowercase: Password must include at least one lowercase letter
errors.passwordNumber: Password must include at least one number
errors.passwordSpecial: Password must include at least one special character (!@#$%^&*)

labels.name: Name
labels.agree: I agree to the terms of use

placeholders.name: Enter your name
placeholders.upload: Drag files here or click to upload

errorBoundary.title: Something went wrong
errorBoundary.description: An unexpected error occurred. Try refreshing the page.

notFound.title: Page not found
notFound.description: The page you are looking for does not exist or has been moved.
